title: Plastic waste generation
subtitle: This measures total plastic waste generation prior to management and therefore does not represent the quantity of plastic at risk of polluting waterways, rivers and the ocean environment.
caption:
Plastic waste generation

This measures total plastic waste generation prior to management and therefore does not represent the quantity of plastic at risk of polluting waterways, rivers and the ocean environment.

The choropleth map displays global plastic waste generation for the year 2010, using a continuous color scale ranging from light beige for zero tonnes to deep maroon for 10 million tonnes. The visualization highlights a concentration of the highest waste volumes in specific large economies, with the United States, China, and Brazil appearing in the darkest red band, indicating generation levels at or near the 10 million tonne maximum. Russia and Germany also feature in the upper tiers of the scale, shaded in dark red tones that correspond to values between 5 million and 10 million tonnes. A secondary tier of moderate generation, represented by medium orange hues falling roughly between 2.5 million and 5 million tonnes, includes nations such as India, Mexico, South Africa, and Australia. Much of the African continent, along with parts of Central Asia and the Middle East, appears in the lightest beige shades, signifying generation figures closer to the 0 to 1 million tonne range. Several regions, including Greenland, Western Sahara, and French Guiana, are marked with diagonal hatching to denote the absence of data. The geographic distribution shows that the largest absolute quantities of plastic waste originate from a distinct set of populous and industrialized nations, while vast areas of the global south and north remain in the lowest quantified bands or lack reported figures entirely.
